speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This interview with Nellie Smith was made at her home in Bovill on September 22nd, 1975.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Before I came here, I began to learn that when I was just a little kid. My mother always did that work. Our mothers and their grandmothers are always doing something like that. They never said, oh, their hands are empty or I'd live there. They're going all the time. They're just, and you're talking. Their hands are into some kind of work. I started to get that pillow slip. I just laid it out here the other day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what were the different kinds of work again?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, there's embroidery and knitting, crochet,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: and... This weaving. They had a little frame board, you know, that they twine onto. We made so many tidies and hot mats and things, hot dish mats. And it was at the time when the CCs were here then that we made those. And maybe you've heard them, the CCs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You made them for the CCs?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, we made them. But after they had balls and balls of twine, great big balls of string, that they'd mark out the spot that they had cleaned from your wild gooseberry.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: What's it called?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Ribes. Ribes. I'd forgotten the name. They'd take it off, you know. Then they'd know where it is. The next fellow would work up to it. They'd try to clean out all the ribes around that area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they had a lot of twine they brought into the area, and that's what you used?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, then they'd go on to the next one. Probably they'd forget, they'd get lost, you know, with their twine. Some of them would go to twine or go to lunch or to supper or something. And the next time, start in someplace else. And I know that a lot of the folks found balls or half balls of that string in the woods where they had been working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would they use that for? For macrame or what? Well, you mean, people here? Yeah, what would you use the twine for?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We used, we had frames, whatever size we wanted. I think that's what they call the macrame now. But we just called it mats, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you make of that? Place mats or what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Anything you wanted. Place mats. And the things for the back of your chairs for protection. And the hot dish mats for those on the table. And, oh, lots of them were, you know, they even made bedspreads of it. And that's where all, you see, the string was put across and then was put the other way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would people ever dye the material first or just use it as the color?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, they used the white bedspread. And I don't know them, there's one lady, her husband, that made it an awfully pretty bedspread, but I'd forgotten. Whether they had it trimmed in any color, a touch of it or not, but it was made of all the time. We found it in the cash in the, or stuck into the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What color was the twine?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, it was, well, it's supposed to be white, but it was kind of a beige white or eggshell type of almost. And, I mean, they'd, or these strings, you know, were laced, or they were put across the nails on each side. And you hooked them on these nails back and forth. And they're about, oh, about, it all depends on how big you wanted your squares between them. You could make them quite a ways apart or you could make them just very small, which is the size you put your nails in there. And then, the ones diagonal, I mean, the longitude and latitude of the thing, and then you go along and hook across the corners of each place where they crossed and tie that knot. And tie that good and strong, and on to the next one, and keep on tying that all solid. And then, you would go to work with the scissors when you got through, turn it over, and take about half of it and clip it with the scissors, and that made a puff ball for each one of those crossings.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Now, isn't that what they call macrame now?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm not sure how macrame is done. I think it's similar. It sure is similar, anyway. Well, I haven't seen any of it or had any experience with it yet. I haven't been out in a very late late.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Rather, been sewing or doing that kind of work. Now, sit still already. Hey, you sit still now. Don't run around.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Where are you going? Are you going in the kitchen?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, that's not the way the kitchen...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: all the time they would make scarves out of it, or they could make, you know, the brownies or anything like that, you know. There was so much of it. It seemed like it was just full of the time.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Everybody was making something of kind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that partly because there was so little money at the time during the Depression?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, it was just something, just lying there, and probably going to waste in time. It was all weather beaten and everything after it was laid there. When they had finished that, they had finished that certain area and left it and gone to the next. Then they had the stichies of young men about that time.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: After they left, of course, they just lay there, and anybody that found one of the cone outfits would just pick it up and bring it on in. Well, the people were tricky enough. They just looked like shame. They didn't lay there any good or not, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think was the most popular of the different handwork? Was knitting the most popular? Is that what you would do the most?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: A whole lot depends on the person. Some people took to knitting, some took to embroidery and all like that. Some people could do all those things. I could embroidery and knit and crochet and tap.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I could do all those things myself. Well, then, there's drawn work, too. All this drawn work. You draw the threads in a piece of material, and then you embroider into those things.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But, no, there was an old lady here. She was getting up in years, but she didn't have to bother her any. She was a live wire, just up and going and all, busy in a bird dog. She was a German lady, and that accounts for part of it. But she said that whenever they... When she was little, her mother taught her to knit. I think she was about four years old. She learned to knit. She said, anytime that their hands were empty, they had nothing to do. They got their knitting and they knitted their own socks. The Muslims had big families, and that's quite a job for one person to knit socks for their husband and the rest of the kids, too. So, she had learned to knit, so she just kept it up. She'd knit socks for her grandkids and all the other ones. Every once in a while, she'd knit a pair for somebody else.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She was quite a hand to watch sports on TV. She'd get her knitting, get out and get herself all fixed up. She'd turn on her TV, and then she'd watch...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, what is this? They have their games?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She'd want to be sure and find out who. She'd sit there and she'd just be knitting to beat the cats a fight. Her hands just flew, and knitting an intricate pattern of a knitted bedspread.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Without looking at it?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He hardly ever looked at it. She just knew what she was doing, and most of the time she'd sit down, but she'd never miss a thing that was going on on that TV. I was over there one afternoon when she was sitting, working at it. She was talking to us, too, at the same time, and I was visiting this other lady and I that went there. Here now, listen, Ernie. Now sit down, or else... I heard the right person was serving on that end. Well, now, come on, sit down somewhere so you won't bother us.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: When...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the work that you did when you were, oh, young, and doing hand work, what would you do? What kinds of things would you make?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I started with hem stitching when I was just... Well, I wasn't that old. Yes, I was just about that size, because we were at grandmothers just before we came out west. We were at grandmothers, or great -grandmothers, and visited with her a little while later. I suppose Mother was kind of making the rounds before she left for the west, because we didn't expect to ever get back. And I had forgotten about it, though, but I had left a little handkerchief at grandmothers, great -grandmothers house, and I had just forgotten it. And when I was about 13, I think about 13, down when I was living out at Troy, we were looking through her trunk when she was living with my aunt right there at the mill. So we laid at the mill, and her dad was working at the mill. And we would look through that and get the kind of board, and she'd say, well, come on, let's go in there and see what we can get. I think I've got so -and -so that she'd be talking about in my own trunk in here. Let's go in there and see what we can find. And of course, I was just my keen delight to look through something like that. But we went in there, and she was talking and explaining everything to me as we went. And she came across this handkerchief and said, here, this is yours. I'll give it to you now. I've had it long enough. And she said, you left it when you were the visitors at that time in Marceline. And she said that Mother had said that I had made that handkerchief when I was six years old. And it has pulled threads, and then the hidden hemstitch.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You know what hemstitching looks like, don't you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Tell me. Well, the threads are all even across the place that they have pulled the threads. Then you take a little bite. Oh, you can do it several ways, but you can have little straight up and down things. And then at the end of the time, you take a little bite with the needle and tie it off and go into the next one and tie it off. Or you can tie that, and then when you put the bottom of it, you don't take the same bunch. You take half of each one. It makes it go zigzag. And mine was that zigzag kind. At the age of six. I can't see how I did it, but Grandma said I did. And she said that Mother said that I had made it. I can remember sitting beside the other one, she'd be sewing. And when I wasn't born in 1903, I think, I was just a kid. But I was, I left lots of little kids. I was always right at her side. And mine wanted to do it too, whatever she was doing. So, she went back one day with, I think Dad had made me a little doll cradle. And she hooked up. She made me a little feather bed for that cradle. And then she fixed the pillow, or bolster, it was in those days as bolsters. And then I had some scraps, you know, she made me a little quilt. And I played with those doll things, I can remember. My sewing machine was just like it was sitting there. And I was sitting right against the wall, right by her, at her side. And she was busy sewing, and I was right down there close. And she'd, of course, she'd have handwork to do too, with her buttonholes and all. And so, she'd give me, to keep me out of her hair, and keep her from pestering her, I guess. She'd give me something to do. Some sewing or something, I could make a doll dress or something. So, I would sew those things, and I can remember that. Just, I can remember the scene. Say, now listen Dad, sit down and sit still. Well, the money was put. Haven't got any, so I had not to worry you any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said you can remember the scene?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Mm -hmm. I can just remember, while they were sitting, I don't remember much about, well, there was a bed over behind, but I can remember that picture in my mind of her working at the sewing machine. And I was down there beside her, and I was sewing whatever little trinkets I had to sew, and she'd give me some things, maybe cut out the doll dress or something like that for me. And I was trying, I don't know what that looked like. And, at that time, I probably, but I was sewing too anyway. I was probably mussy sewing up those things. I don't remember them.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: This is even before you were six.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh yes, that was just when I was a little tyke, and just in her hair all the time, and sew too. So, I know that I was doing the other transfer, but she let me do that, and until I got, of course, I was learning. I was learning to use my needle and thread and all by working there with Mother so much.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I had, or, I had, what was it? It was like that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Anyhow, I was just learning that the, like, it's clumsy for a little kid just starting, you know, their needle doesn't go where they want it to and all. I guess eventually I kept on, I suppose, improved all the time, so I kept so I could sew it. But they, I have that little old handkerchief here, and Grandma gave it to me. I didn't remember at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember doing much work as you were growing up out here in the West? Do you remember you doing very much?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh yes, we had it in school on the first school we had here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Bovill?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, it was the second, the second school that we had had here. And, oh yeah, every Friday afternoon I would have sewing. The first year we had Mrs. Bovill came in and she read books to us. She read, oh, those books would really be most interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was, what's his name?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I wrote the Freckles and some of those books. And she'd come and read as many chapters as she could before the time was up. And she read those books clear through to us that day. And that was interesting, but.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did you already know how to, when you took up the handwork in school, did you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You already knew how to do it pretty well.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, at that time. Because I was making a lot of my own clothes at that time.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, there's a picture that's out there this morning. It's laying over there, I know the blouse. I noticed that I was looking at that, I thought, well, there's my blouse. I was getting pretty catty with the sewing machine and cutting and everything at that time. I had a white indie linen blouse that I had made and little tiny fine tucks across the yoke and little patches of them. And then the puff sleeve with the little cuff here and the lace at the end of it. And I'm obsessed about all of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you need a pattern to make something like that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I did at the time, but I soon got it, so I didn't need any pattern any longer. After I was married and got to doing more of it all the time, well, maybe some before I was married, but I don't know what the pattern was.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Before that, I had cut my own patterns, most of them, because I'd kind of sized up about what I wanted. And then I'd maybe take measurements if it's for somebody else, so I could do measurements with the tape measure and I could cut that and cut some of those patterns myself. Most of them I just went kind of by guess, or not exactly by guess either, but maybe I'd laid down a garment that did fit, but just about what I wanted. And I cut kind of by that. And you can't get an accurate pattern with that unless you're pretty catty with the pins and the scissors.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I made all the wedding clothes for my, well, my granddaughter was living here with us. See, her parents had separated. And I inherited the three kids. She wouldn't let him have them, he wouldn't let her have them. But they agreed to let me have them. So, I kind of made all of her clothes as she grew up. Everybody always said, well, she said, well, now that kid just looks like he just stepped out of a band box or something like that. And you always make those things look so tailored, you know, like it's never just my store.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, well, hey, that's no trick, are they?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Just wipe them out and go at it. It was kind of easy, but it was easier for me to do than them. I suppose because I'd practiced it a long time. And I liked it. That's one thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were growing up, was that usual? Did most of the kids have their clothes made at home or making themselves?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Most everything was made at home.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And the mother I know used to do sewing for one of the ladies in town. She had quite a large family. And they're awfully good friends of ours. She's always sending over a bunch of material for the mother to make up. She would try to do it herself sometimes. But she would, I don't know, instead of making a felt seam or something like that, you know, that smooth seam. She would have half a dozen different thicknesses piled up so she'd make a notch in over the seams and across and all. She was trying off her heart and she was doing the best she could, but she just didn't know how. She wasn't as used to it as I was. But I just kind of grew up knowing because mother was that way and I was in the midst of it all the time. But now my sister wasn't. She didn't take to it so much. And I guess maybe mother didn't, she probably didn't bother mother so much about it. She was very young and she was very young. And we'd have to kind of give her some rags to keep her out of her hair. That was like she was me. I guess because Edna, my sister, was, well, they told a joke on her that in Houston they used a lot of flour sacks. Mother made me a whole outfit. She made me the underskirt, you know, the underskirt had a bend around the waist and then the skirt went on down and there's a flounce. And then the flounce, the gathered ruffle went on to us about at the knees like that. And there was a gathered fold from there on down. And that had little tucks in that flounce and she had, I think she had crocheted lace on the bottom of it too. I don't remember whether that was crocheted lace or whether it was some store lace.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But, so Edna and the little girl would leave her to us, took an ocean and they were going to sew something. And they didn't like about it. Now my sister passed away but the other one, I saw her at the picnic not so long ago. And she asked me if I remembered the sewing that she and Edna did. And I said yes and I just can't forget it either. She gets a big kick out of it and I have to tell her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They said they were going to sew and they'd go out to the playhouse and they were going to sew. And they asked my mother -in -law what they were going to do with it. They each had a flour sack. Well, they didn't want to tell her. They didn't want to show her when she got through. So she was alone and I don't remember where they had any pattern. I think maybe mother cut out a half pattern out of a newspaper for them. I'm not sure how she gave it to them. And they were supposed to be a pair of panties. And they had the hems at the bottom and the gathers on the belt at the top. So they worked real hard. And of course if it had a piece of material about that right at the top, that had to be gathered up so to fit the belt up and sew on. Well, they gathered it up and when they got it through, they had to put it on plain. Then when they got to the end of it, all those gathers went in one bunch.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Instead of making it even all the way around the gathers, they put it all together plain and then there's a whole bunch of gathers right at the one side. The joke was, that was pretty funny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, anyhow, when they come in and fussing about it, well what's the matter with these things?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She hadn't asked any questions before and she didn't know what to do about it. And it didn't look like she thought they ought to. Anyhow, I was in a hurry for school one morning and I grabbed all those pants and put them on and hiked up to a hurry to school. And I came home fussing at noon. I was just about to have a fit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I thought, what's the matter?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, well this pesky pants that had been made. And I said, I was about to cross legged and fall down. And I got those off the scooters. I got a hold of them. I reached in the cupboard and got a hold of them. And I thought they had my pair and I was in such a hurry, I didn't think that was mine. And Mother and Edna just laughed while they were sleeping. I told them what a time I'd had with those things. I said, I was afraid I'd fall down every time I took a step. And Georgie was the other girl and she just laughed every time she thinks how their sewing turned out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The way they were set up, they would really just fall down off you all the time?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, yes, you could get the band pinned or whatever it was. They were safe. But here was all your getters in one place and there was a playing room.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But I can say, I was 16 or 17.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: 16 I think. When Mother was sewing for other people quite a lot. And the girl worked up at the restaurant. Her mother had run the restaurant. And her name was Bean. Ernie Bean. Ernestine Bean. And she brought up some material and wanted Mother to make her some dresses for her to wait tables in. She helped her mother in the restaurant. And Mother told her she just didn't have time. She had so much on her hands she just couldn't do it. And just then anyhow, she couldn't. And I was showing her one of my dresses I had just finished.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she said, Mother, you made that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, yes.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she said, why don't you make my dresses then?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, I've never been sold to anybody else. I was scared to. And she said, just go ahead and make them. She said, that's good enough for me. And I made her. She wanted dresses made out of black sateen. And they were trimmed around the edge of the belt. There was a little piping.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what the piping is?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The edge of it shows just like a trimming.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Like this would be. And the edge of it was bound like a little binding. And the little blue edge is showing. You know, this little speck of blue. And the other was too soon.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I can't remember what the other was. But anyhow, she wanted to make the shirt waist style and the plain skirts. And the button holes from the neck to the bottom. And I used to make all those button holes by hand. And I'd hate to have to do it now. But that's what I had. That's what she wanted. So that's what I made. I took the job and made her two suits. And she had to fit a bound. She thought they were so nice. That's what she wanted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did it take you to do something like that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, I don't know. I guess a few days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have your own sewing machine?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, I was whenever my mother was using it. I would. I used to have her sewing machine. That's when he bought for me in 1913. That was the first year we were married. And the machine was the thing. The machine agent came along and we proceeded to get one. We were going to move to ourselves. We were still building this house. And we were living with mother over at their place yet. We were living at our house. I was pregnant and was doing a lot of sewing. He bought the machine for me.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It was the 5th of August that we got it. My baby was born on the 30th of August. It was just about quite a month after that. But I was just sewing like a storm. I was sewing like a storm there. Because, well, mother was... And my sister was taking lessons, music lessons at the time. It had been from Mrs. Clickner. Her husband was the depot agent here and she lived up on that hill by McDonald's. And she had been teaching this class quite a while. And she had arranged for a recital on Saturday night. So, the mother and my aunt lived near us over there. And she was to go with the recital that night. So, they were supposed to go at 9 o 'clock. So, it used to be known that an Italian lady, I think she was, or some foreign person, that used to peddle things through the town. She'd come through every once in a while with yards and yards of dress materials and all fancy tidies and paintings and things to sell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Paintings?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, they were, I suppose, done by machinery or something. And she, Dora, I think she called her. I think that was her name. She'd just come with the biggest suitcases here. So, you wonder how in the world she could possibly carry them. But she came and she liked it. She'd been there before several times. So, you always stop and talk to her and looked at all the pretty things she'd had, sometimes bought. And she was real keen about just felt at home with our place. So, anyhow, that was just a week before my baby came. So, then I started out. She came there. Well, I pitched that way. She said, not that way. You can have your new dress for the recital up there. And I was going to pitch in and make them. So, I pitched in and made the three dresses for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Three dresses?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For your sister? For your?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Mother.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, one, two, three, yes. It was about my sister, Edna, and Aunt Mary, and mother. Aunt Mary was the large one, about 225 or 250. And she had a light, rather light blue, a pretty blue dress. And it was the full -length dress and had, I guess, just about, you know, that's where they were in about that length then. I don't remember so much how it was made, but I think, oh, I know, they had that kind of an apron -like thing, a tunic, I figure, that was pointed in front and then shaped up toward the back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did this one week before you had your child?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But I was having fun. I was just hurrying like everything because I just couldn't, I wasn't going to get those three dresses up. I bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the only, the main way to get material then from this lady, or did they have much in town?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, they didn't have anything like that. She carried quite a lot of fancy materials. And that was the part that had a silky flower in it, a little woven in, you know, little fine flowers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she go from town to town, this lady?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, she went all to this part of the country, everybody knew her. Did she stop by the house and say, is that how you... Yes, she was about to come right to the house.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, we always welcomed her, she'd come on in, and she felt at home, so she would come in and talk and laugh, but she could, you know, but she didn't talk real plain, all of it, but we can understand her. But that dress I made for Aunt Mary, I think hers had that little silk, kind of fringy edgy on it, on that part of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the tunic?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: On the tunic, yeah. And I got hers done and made Edna's, and she was kind of slender, her own slender. And hers was a tangerine color, and it was made, I think, but the seam went down the side here, and this one was puffed up to suit, you know, kind of a slush.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the front of the slush?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, in the side front, and this piece was caved up here, and that was straight, a lot of them were made that way then.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And there was some kind of a droop, there was the collar there of it, come across this way, and then the other piece, like we were, like that. And hers had the lacy fringe on that collar.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It sounds very fancy.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They were real dressy dresses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have patterns for those?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Just had the common patterns that we would use for things like that, and wherever. I wanted something else, so I just put it on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a pattern for each, the size that each one was, or did you know the size?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I just take your measurements with a tape measure and then add to or take from. Maybe pin a plate in if it was going to be too large for the other, add to it. And then, Mother's, and this was that kind of, had the surplus.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Surplus and the top.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And my mother's, hers was black taffeta. And it was made to leave kind of a panel in the front here. And those panels were plated in there like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pleated right in the front?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Just across here. But this piece in here was a lacy, some kind of a lace front.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had lace in it in the collar?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh. Well this was across here and down to the waistline. Across the front. It was, I think, white lace in there. But these, the other, the edge of her, the other thing where it met the lace had a pleat like your shirt front. Like that. It went down on each side of it. Well that was, that was piped in deep rose color. Kind of shed off so much of that black, you know. And that was piped in that rose color and piped on the sleeves, on the cuffs, and on the belt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well now, where did you get the ideas for the design? Were they just dresses that were being worn at the time?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, they were. I think of a magazine or catalog or something.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was telling them I like, but that's caveats usually. That's what I do. I find something I like in the catalog, but I go ahead and make it. I don't do much anymore. I never get time to sew and I'm about to forget how.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you make all three dresses before you had your kit?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I got mothers, I got mothers all done. And I was working on the day of the recital. I was working right up to the time and I was scared I wasn't going to get it done. I was in a hurry and I was like everything. So part of it, I didn't get stitched solid or something. I know I had to almost leave part of it. I had to leave it to be finished afterwards. I got picked on everything, but I think the special tools I had, I know the books and the ideas were going to be, I had to leave those off and I put pins in for the recital. But I got it now so she could wear her dress. I got her hair fixed and I sometimes had to help with that. I think she probably did herself that time. And she took her, she wore the dress in and went to the party. It was about 12 o 'clock. I came back home and I was sitting by the fire. But it was, I had started to have a party myself at that time.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And when the mother came in the door she said, what are you doing up here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, oh, me folks like to go party, so maybe I'll give you another one. I just kidded with them.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, why are you sick?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, don't. I felt I would never go better in my life. But I had indications it showed that I would be trouble. And it probably would be. Probably things would finish up alright. And she wanted to, she wanted to hustle around and she turned to Evelyn and says, well, let me back up. My aunt just lived two, three doors from the south of the street. I said, well, I'm up there and catch that Mary before she gets in bed and tell her to come on down. That we might need her here. And I was laughing and kidding with them. Once in a while I would have a little kink. It wasn't so good, but I didn't pay attention.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, I'd had lots worse pains than that, but it was fine. Eating cabbage and vinegar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were what? Five? When you were what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, I told her I'd had lots worse pains than that, than I was having then. And I said, well, for eating cabbage with vinegar on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was a, what do you mean eating cabbage with vinegar on it? That was a worse pain?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, that was sometimes it gives people cramps, you know. But yes, and that was, I said, well, I've had lots worse than that just for eating. So,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I figured, well, it's good to know it when I got the real thing because I'd heard others talk about it. And one lady, one of her close neighbors had just had a baby two or three weeks before that.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she said, she called the doctor in, or something called the doctor in, and he told her, well, she said in this case, and said, what about the good things she held when she did?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she said, well, she said, whenever you begin to have those regular pains, five minutes apart, you can look for a youngster within the hour. And so, she said, I think she said it worked out that way too with her in less than an hour. But when I thought, well, that sounds funny for a doctor to say that because that can't be my part, my deal. I was told the way I would be because I thought I'm not ready yet. But they kept wanting me to go for the doctor. I said, well, I don't need him. There's nothing now, but I don't think I need a doctor now. I feel fine. Well, then they'd all come around and have the fit come, say, go get the doctor. And finally, I figured it would get a little tougher. So I said, well, you felt a little bitter, but we couldn't get him.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, they did. And the first thing about it, it was probably like there was a door there, and he came in that side door and had taken off his gloves and set his little black bag down on the chair nearby there. And my bed was off here. There was just a curtain across the door. And the mother had the lamp lit in there and no electricity then, you know. So, she had the light lit in that bedroom. And he was standing there facing that way, talking to each other. I was standing on this side over here where the stove was, and I was standing in behind the stove, just room enough I could squeeze through between the stove and the wall. And I got some of this chili, and I was kind of freezing. It was the deal working on me. I remember I was getting shaky in chili, so I got in there where it was warm. And then I used to end up talking to him, and he started to pick up this bag and go out in high sneakers. It was funny, like, he kind of giggled through there in return to see what happened. And he looked funny. I said, well, I guess I'm not the one. I guess I'm the one you're looking for, maybe. Not me, but not the one you should expect to find me here, or something like that. I said, but I guess I'm the one you're looking for. Oh, well, all right. And he wanted to know how I was, so I said, well, fine, so far.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He hadn't even really noticed you until?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, he'd seen me before. He'd been expecting that gesture for two months. All the way, he told me it was about the 5th of August.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did you say to him?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You said you're... I said, well, I guess I don't look like the one you're looking for, but I guess I am, or something like that, I said.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he said, come back then, and don't talk to me. And he said something about being so far apart and everything, and as severe as he. And I said, no, they weren't that tall. I had gone then and sat in the rocking chair in front of the stove.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he's watching me. And finally, he said,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know about all the timing,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: the timing, it's a little bit about a minute or two minutes apart, or something like that. And he looked like he didn't believe it. So he began timing me. He took out his watch and began timing me. Once in a while, I kind of screamed my face up and was kind of more severe.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He watched that for a while. He thought I didn't know the time that I was sitting facing the clock on the wall. And I did happen to notice the timing. So after he had checked your three of those, there wasn't any intermission between them, apparently. And so he said, I think we'd better get you in here and find out what's going on. So he took me in the bedroom there, and they came up down high. This was how long it would take to get some boiling hot water, get these things going. He says, it's already boiling. And I said, well, good. He said, get these instruments down. You're going to cook as quick as you can. He was just stirring around, hurrying around everything. The baby's already on the way then.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So he gave her a lecture, and then he came back to me. And he said, I said, now, I'm going to tell you, I see trouble on the hands here. But we'll have to avoid it all we can. And he said, you're going to have a brush presentation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: A buttocks presentation. He thought there was going to be something here. And he said, one foot is appearing now.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I thought, I need to tell you, you need a lecture. He said, once this all starts, I'm sorry to tell you, we can't give you a speck of ether or anything. I said, once this all starts, you have to work just fast and hard. Because once this starts, we've got five minutes to get this over with, or we probably have a dead baby. So I said, all right, I'm game. I said, I'll do what I can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you afraid?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, I wasn't. I thought, well, I used to be kind of in the first part of that. But there's been thousands of them born for my baby, so I'll make it through too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say the first part, when you were first with child? And I was kind of thinking then, well, how can it all be?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But then I finally figured out, I thought, well, there's been thousands of them, and hardly any of them ever lived, I mean, every died in childbirth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you in pain at this point when you were lying in bed there? Was it hurting you pretty bad?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, not so bad. It was just the remission and the sun. But anyhow, he said to me, just five minutes to spare during that time. And I thought, I don't want that. I've done most anything because I was so afraid that anyone might have a dead baby. And that baby was pretty precious to me, and I wanted it to be all right. So after a while, we were boarding. We had some boarders that were staying in the little shack we had on the back. It was a little, it was a perfectly good little house, but just a small place. And they had rented it for, they were taking pictures or enlarging pictures or something like that. And they had rented that for us. So I was so scared that I'd make a lot of fuss. And I heard people just screaming and yelling. And the surgeons were in the hospital making such a fuss. And I just forget a death, I'd do it that way. And I didn't want those fellows to hear me hollering around. So anyhow, it came down, he went back in to tell the mother something. And when he came back in here by the haze of the hurry, they said that the feet were presenting. And they had some, they'd help him get his guard up on and tie his apron and give his gloves. And I don't think everything gets gloves on. They just, the baby came straight now. And then they began saying, well, we can get Miss Smith, now hold back Miss Smith. Well, wait a minute. I'm just kidding. It was coming too fast then, but that was easier said than done.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Two minutes, my baby was there. And it came all right. That was another baby born just a week later, a distant girl. And born in the same position, same thing happening. And they broke her collarbone. And getting there, her little arm was kinked up somewhere. And they kind of had her taped up for quite a while.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But mine came fine, just wonderful. It sounds like he was more nervous than you were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It sounds like he was more nervous than you were. Yeah.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, the doctor, he was a good doctor. And he just told me that there are facts. Well, I think he knew too that I was pretty much out and out, whatever the name was, to face it. And do what I'm supposed to. And that's what I did.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Was this Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gibson? Do you think that it was different with some women? Did some women have much more trouble, I mean, facing this kind of a thing?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, yes they do sometimes.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Some of them, 25, 30 hours sometimes in labor. And that's no fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In those days when they were having babies at home, it was the same way?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Long labor sometimes?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, some of them.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I couldn't be telling you all this stuff. I don't suppose it was a very proper conversation.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, it's the way people lived in those days. I think it's important to know.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But another one of our neighbors lived over there. She was quite a chum of mine. And she was expecting. And she did have a 12 -pound baby in the long run. And the doctor was there and she had worked and worked and she was worn out. And it had been quite a long time, the stretch, and he had told her now, you must work hard with this next pain or something like that. And she must bear down. And she just had to work hard with that. And she would just give up and she'd say, well, I can't, I've done all I can do. And she was just kind of relaxed, she didn't give up. And finally he just slapped her, right, alongside the face. A very good son's flap. And she got so mad at him she could get up. She could have done it. She got up and tied him in a shoulder shoe or anything. She was just so mad. And he let her go and she went to work pinned and she just was all stirred up. And she was there a little while. And she was all that over. After he came, he apologized to her. He said, no, I said, my apology not to you. I said I had to do that. He said that some people just have to, if they just get worn out and give up. You have to make them mad. Make them mad or slap or something. Make them mad and they'll tend to go to work stronger.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she said, well, you don't know how that hurts. She said, oh, yes, but I do. She said, yes, like fun you do. And he said, yes, I do. I dreamed one night I had a baby and I was never in such misery. And all my life it wasn't that dream. And so she got over her man's pill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this the same doctor?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, the same doctor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the way most all the women had their kids at home?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. They didn't use the hospital then.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, some of them did, but I didn't want to go to the hospital. I just wanted my youngsters at home and I had to marry the whole six of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When the baby was born then, he just stayed with you at home and you just were supposed to lie in bed and recuperate? Is that how it worked?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, he was supposed to stay in bed for ten days anyhow. That was the day that everybody considered it. That had been a kind of a hand -me -down idea from way back.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Sometimes 12 days, but he was supposed to stay anyhow ten days in bed. The tenth day, everything was supposed to fall back in its place and it would all become normal again. I said, of all things, everything was not going to just wait until the tenth day to jump back in.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I would get wicked in the dickens not doing anything.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, I think it was the full ten days at that time. I didn't feel like it. I felt like getting up and going to work or something, but that was the rule at that time. He was supposed to stay in there. Then when I had the next to last one, I was in bed six days. Then he picked me up at the hospital, took my baby girl up there. Because I had trouble with her and they were going to operate on her. She had, I don't remember now the name of the use, but anyhow, they performed an osteopathy. Let me see. Anyhow.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Anyhow, she had a malformation of her bowel.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She had been lying there, beautiful baby. And it was a really cozy, cuddly little thing, you know. Beautiful child. But she didn't have the, well, I don't know whether it was something that was missing. They thought it was either a malformation near the rectum. It was either a disconnection or it was a knotted or something. Nothing could pass. She had been lying in bed there with me and here at home. And she had been taking her feed all the time. But she began, the last two days, burping it up. There was some kind of stoppage there that prevented going on through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they took her and you both to hospital?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They took her up there to operate. That's one of the hardest things I ever did. So I didn't take her up there to operate. And there was a little tiny thing, six days old. And I thought, well, they said that she only had a 50 -50 chance of success if they took her there. And she would have to have another operation later. But she maybe was five years older and she was old enough, you know, to stand.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And then she wouldn't have any chance at all, they didn't think, without it. So there I was, deciding to send that baby up there and let them cut into her and think there's probably no chance for her. And then I think, well, if they had a 50 -50 chance, maybe I'd better lean on that little bit of hope.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And they took her up there and she had, she was just six days old, I think, and they took her up there. They came down and got me, I sent Mother up to hospital with her. So when Mother was here, I said, you go and stay right there with her and see that everything is done right. Take good care of her. Mother was there. And then that evening she came down and I said,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: the hospital is right up there, you know, where that building is up there, where the halls, the apartments, the apartment house. It was a hospital, and she came down and she said, well, you know, I'm not doing a thing up there, I just sit there with my hands falling and there's nothing I can do for the baby. So she says, why don't you go up there and stay with her and let me be down here and I can be doing some work down here. And I said, well, I didn't know. She said the doctor said that it would be alright, I could do. So he came down and got me and took the car, took me up there. She died that day, she was 11 weeks old.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I held a grudge kind of against the doctor at that time. Because in the morning, as she took the worse, of course I wasn't supposed to pick her up or do anything for her really. But I could give her a sip of water or something like that. And so I was watching carefully, I was following orders. And I noticed that morning she was having more trouble breathing and she was worse.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I called the nurse, she came upstairs and said, well, she's, yes, she's, I know, I said, did she need anything more to eat now?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, she acts like she's awfully, or a little nervous and she might act like she might be hungry. Maybe if she had a bit to eat, it might come. Well, I shaved you a while ago. You've been a dreamin', I just got through shaving you before you ate.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so, and so the nurse went back down and said, no, she don't need anything now, she's had her regular time for her feed and on schedule and when it comes time, we'll give it to her. And so she left me and I thought, well, I was very consoling, but I assessed her right by her and watched her closely. And finally I asked her if she wouldn't call the doctor then, he just lived up in the next place above it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she wouldn't call the doctor, I said, see, there wasn't something that he could do, maybe a peach, you know, something, an emergency.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she was supposed to call him, I don't know whether she did or not, she said she did, but he didn't come. It was about four o 'clock in the morning. And she, so he didn't go ahead and I asked her later on if she, you know, if he wasn't coming or if he couldn't, I was expecting him to come right on down and consider an emergency. And she said that she had to wait to eat his breakfast first before he came down.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He had said he was going to wait and see if the baby would eat or she wanted to wait and see?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, I was going to see if the baby could have something to eat, but she said no, there wasn't time for the feeding, she was going for that. And there wasn't time for it, but as time came by, she got it. So I asked her to call the doctor and have him to check her condition in general, because I could see she was worse.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So he didn't come, he didn't wait, he didn't show up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was the second time you'd asked her?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he didn't come, so it went on until about seven o 'clock in the morning or something like that, me and everybody showed up. And so,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I found out the baby had died. I didn't ask him the second time until I could see she was going fast then. And I took the baby up and held her in my arms. It kind of elevated, you know, gave her better room to breathe by elevating her head and holding her in a slanted position.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so, he didn't come, so I asked the nurse then, I said, what do they usually do in case of this kind here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, she's gone now. Well, at first, when she was bad and was going, the nurse looked at her and said, oh, she needs changing. I'll get here and change her and fix her up or something like that. I said, no. Just let her alone. And I said, she's suffering enough. I said, it's just not. I said, it'll soon be over.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, what, that little bit of must that reason put her in her double?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, but rather you don't. You just let her alone. It would be as comfortable as you can. And, well, all right if you say so.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so I said, so, I said, what do they usually do afterwards when she was gone? I said, what do they usually do here in case of that kind? And I said, do they just leave her lay here on the bed until he gets here or what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She says, well, no, they usually take him down to the basement. And I just thought, you're not taking him down to the basement. I didn't say anything to her. I asked her to get the janitor there. He was the boy I'd known for a long time. I'd been going to school with him, I think. I'd known him for a long while here.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, I said, Joe, would you go down and tell her you're coming up?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I think the baby is dying now. And he just hustled right down and got back with Ernie. And she was gone by the time he got back. And that's when I asked Peggy what they usually did. So I sat and waited a little while. I used to live with maybe... I took her things and packed up my suitcase.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I think about seven o 'clock came. And I said, Ernie, if you can carry these suitcases, let's go home. And I said, I can carry the baby. It was only 11 days after. And I was ordinarily under those conditions. They would have thought I shouldn't be, you know, packing around. They would have hurt me. But I carried my baby in the blanket and carried her home. Walked down from that hospital and carried her home herself. And you know, that doctor never did see that baby after she passed away. He doesn't know where she lived or died. Except the nurse, the nurse said so. He had the nurse's word for it. And he never did... He thought he would have made some apology to me or some bit of condolences of some kind. But never heard.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He just brought her down to the house here and laid her out here. The house.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, that was a trouble. The two days after the operation, they had had to go in there again. And they said they had made too large an incision. A little bit too... And they had to go in there and... I don't know what time... They closed that little, I think, or something like that. And they had given her...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: What is it?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It wasn't ether, but it was... Some other anesthetic they had given her. And they thought she was too delicate and too little to... Too much of a strain, the whole thing, in general.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So... It seems really... It must have been very hard on you during that time. Mm -hmm. It was. And deciding whether to send her out there, knowing what they were going to do, and knowing it was serious. There's probably no chance. I just felt like you'd leave a little lamb out to slaughter her.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She's the only girl that you ever had. Oh, yeah. All you had was some boys.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I had five boys and one girl.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: When you had babies at home, when they were first born, were there certain precautions you were supposed to take about... About the first...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The first days and the first weeks?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Were there certain ways of doing things that the mothers were supposed to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For her or for the young ones?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, for the kids, for whatever, for the average baby that was born in those days at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Since the baby wasn't in the hospital and they didn't take care of him for you, were there certain rules that the mothers were supposed to follow?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they all grew up knowing that, I think. They were handed down from mother to mother, from grandmothers and all. Many of them were born with a midwife at attention. That's all there was to it. The midwife took care of the case.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Midwife would stay at the home for a living?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, she'd just go and come, I think.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Sometimes if it was handy or if they lived a long ways away, they would stay. I didn't happen to run into any of those. Of course, he was here. The doctor was here at the time.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Lindsay, she was my fifth and then I just had one more. That was a boy. He wasn't here for that.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He had gone and been transferred to Pop -Hatch, I think.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He had given his turn over his book to the doctor that was in charge, Dr. Ona Sorg. He was, I guess, a big detriment or something. He had been in charge of the Naval Hospital for, I think he'd been in the Navy for about 20 or 25 years. I think it was 25 that he'd been the Navy doctor. When he got here, Dr. Gibson had his book all filled up with, oh, there's an awful lot of things that are going on here. I had it all filled up with that. I went up one time for a checkup when I was expecting Lloyd. I went up to see him the first time. He said something about Dr. Gibson leaving. He said, I never saw so many babies in one place in my life. He said, I can just see, well, he said, the tender mother thought I was getting this down. I can just see his big old grin that's coming out of his chin. He always pictured him as a big shiny grin.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He'd be grinning because there were so many babies.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He was always in favor of large families and loved kids, I guess. Teddy Roosevelt. Teddy Roosevelt. Teddy thought I'd been here at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have the idea then that if a baby cried, you were supposed to pick him up?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, they didn't want you to. Hardly, but I kind of, it is a place. I don't know if you are, in any case, the evidence at all. I know you're youngsters. I know a few things about life. You're going to know when you cry if something's the matter with them, or whether you're just hungry or something else. It's a different cry. You already know that cry.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Sometimes they have cramps in their stomach, and you generally turn them over on their stomach and pass them a little bar, and that helps them to put oil sand over your shoulder and brings up the burp or two, and sometimes it'll heal the whole thing.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And they don't want you to pick him up. There is such a thing as picking up a baby every time it moves and spoiling it to death, and they'll cry then just to get up. But people kind of learn that, know whether there's a different tone of voice and all. Generally, this woman over here, she's giving her a sputter now, I think. It's only four weeks old. She was over here the next day or so after the baby was born, and she was born in the hospital over there. But she was up and going around, had the baby on her arm, and this was fine. They just let him come home the second day. So, things have changed considerably, and I think the girls are really healthier. They don't lose their strength, you know, like they do if you just have lay down in the light there for ten days. They get up the next day after the baby's born or something like that. Maybe if it's born during the night, you can give up the next morning, and send them to the bathroom, and go back to bed, just up and down, and they don't lose their strength at all.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I know there used to be, oh, I've heard people say stuff like, one of the old ideas was that you had to be careful not to let a cat near a baby because a cat could really hurt the baby. Is that something that you learned when you... Yes, I've heard that a good deal.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They never let the cat that they've hurt, you know, and remember how they'll work with their claws. They knead. Uh -huh, kind of kneading like, and they said that they can't stop the baby's breath, you know, because they're just as tender and gives a lot, and they said that they could just, that's what their theory was, anyhow.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I've seen them to watch them offcoast, and let the cat get to the youngster, but we want a cat that's up there with the youngster anyway.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So... This woman over here, she picked up her baby.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Do you know what it looks like around it, over here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She's been over here two or three times now since her baby came. Just the other day, she was here and the baby was a month old and four weeks old, and she had this one arm of a blanket just wrapped in a little seating blanket, and the little cute little doves here, just pretty and plump and, you know, even features and just real pretty, and never says a word, never, so they just contain it as can be, and never seems to, another day it did start to wimper a little bit and fidget and cry, and she said it's time for eating again. It's dinner time, she knows it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do mothers used to be careful when you were a mother? Did you have to be careful about taking the kid out and that sort of thing, or would you do that with babies, take them with you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They'd be careful right from the beginning, because the little whiff of air, fresh air, they weren't used to it, and they'd give them a little whiff or two of that fresh air in their face, and they'd have the colic.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I think that could be a bad if you put a big warm head, you have butter bottle or something, you know, on the blanket, you know, right under them, you know, as you turn them on their stomach, and there's something warm with their feet, doesn't it generally get over it a little hard?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Or sometimes a little bit of, oh, just a drop or two of peppermint or something like that in sweetened water. But generally just give them a bottle, as warm as they could stand, just a big warm water, and what they'd drink of that would really do it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I wanted to ask you about that fire that came down from Beals Butte and almost took the town with it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, that was, I just think, you know, I kind of get sidetracked in the doctor's business.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, I enjoyed that very much, because it helps you understand how different things were in those days. Now they, oh, it's all so different. The mother that wants to have her kid at home is looked at as being pretty... Well, it's backwards or something, I guess. Kind of backwardsy. Well, I think it's the way everyone did it, but still it's history now that we're having each other. Many of them had that.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The youngsters were born on the covered wagon on the way to Northwest or something, or sometimes the mother died and she didn't have milk feeding it. I don't know if someone I heard talk about that they were raised on mayor's milk on the crossing of the plains. They had a mayor in their court that they were taking along there, and that mayor first, or that mother got sick or something, I don't know. It's an emergency at home. It's been a long time. There's a lot of things a person can do in a pinch if they just know about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother attended by a midwife when she had her kids, do you know?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know. No.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Because she said the doctor's name who took care of me when I was born. I think Dr. Brushes was her. I don't know. I just don't know about it, but I'm afraid I mentioned him, Dr. Brushes.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know that maybe grandmother, I don't know, probably, had midwife there since my mother was born. That was back in 1868.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was going to ask you when you talked about all the needlework that your mother did.

words: Did she get an income from doing that or a small income from doing that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No. She just did it. Yeah, she did it for her own self.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She made a lot of things. All her daughters and things were trimmed up and did work on them and all. And then if anything, she's made it for anybody else. I don't know that she did that kind of work hardly for anyone else. But if it did, it was a gift, something that she was finding on and making for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that sort of skill really a prized skill? Was that something that was considered?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, no. You scratch up some of the old pieces from that time and show them nowadays, and probably it's something to hand them down, something worth asking.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: That's one reason I was going to show you the pillow slips that I started to make. I got one of them finished since I was 80 years old. And then the other one I hadn't even started yet. I was too busy. I said, I'll get busy. I'll run out of time.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But now you take some of those old -fashioned things and bring them up in their own display in museums and things.
